did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning U-Net approach for the segmentation of telangiectasia (blood vessels) in dermoscopic images of basal cell carcinoma, a type of skin cancer. It employs a combination loss function (weighted binary cross-entropy and Dice loss) to handle class imbalance and compares performance against human observer variability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It describes a specific methodology (deep learning U-Net approach) for detecting blood vessels in basal cell carcinoma, details the dataset used (HAM10000 and NIH studies), reports on hyperparameters and loss functions, and presents new quantitative results (Jaccard scores, accuracy, precision, recall) comparing the model's performance against human observer variability.

title: A deep learning approach to detect blood vessels in basal cell carcinoma
brief_summary: This study automates the segmentation of telangiectasia (blood vessels) in dermoscopic images of basal cell carcinoma using a deep learning U-Net approach. The researchers utilized a combined dataset of 690 images from the HAM10000 and NIH studies, applying extensive preprocessing and data augmentation techniques. To address severe class imbalance between vessel and skin pixels, the model employed a combination loss function consisting of weighted binary cross-entropy and Dice loss. The primary evaluation involved comparing the model's pixel-based detection performance against human observer variability in annotation. The results demonstrated that the proposed approach achieved Jaccard scores within the range of human observer variation, establishing a baseline for automatic identification of cancer-specific structures.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses exclusively on dermoscopic images of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) to detect telangiectasia (blood vessels). The dataset consists of 690 images from the HAM10000 and NIH studies, specifically selected for BCC.

all_task_types.target: Telangiectasia (blood vessels) in dermoscopic images of basal cell carcinoma
all_task_types.details: The paper automates the segmentation of telangiectasia using a deep learning U-Net approach. It performs pixel-based classification to generate binary masks for vessel detection, addressing class imbalance between skin and vessel pixels. The model outputs pixel-wise predictions (presence or absence of vessels) evaluated using Jaccard index, accuracy, precision, and recall.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image dataset of common pigmented skin lesions by Tschandl et al., containing images of size 450 × 600.

use.training: Included in the combined dataset of 690 BCC images used for training, validation, and testing.
use.validation: Included in the combined dataset of 690 BCC images used for training, validation, and testing.
use.testing: Included in the combined dataset of 690 BCC images used for training, validation, and testing.

datasets_used.name: NIH Studies (R43 CA153927-01 and CA101639-02A2)
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic image datasets from NIH studies, containing images of size 768 × 1024.

use.training: Included in the combined dataset of 690 BCC images used for training, validation, and testing.
use.validation: Included in the combined dataset of 690 BCC images used for training, validation, and testing.
use.testing: Included in the combined dataset of 690 BCC images used for training, validation, and testing.

datasets_used.name: Combined BCC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset of 690 dermoscopic images of basal cell carcinoma from HAM10000 and NIH studies, processed to square dimensions (512 × 512) and augmented.

use.training: Used for training the U-Net model (445 images). After augmentation, this contributed to 3114 training images.
use.validation: Used for validation (112 images). After augmentation, this contributed to 784 validation images.
use.testing: Used for testing the model (133 images).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Task 1 Lesion Segmentation Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset used to train U-Net for automatic lesion border determination. Images were resized to 320 × 320.

use.training: Used to train U-Net for lesion border segmentation (80:20 split for training and validation).
use.validation: Used to validate U-Net for lesion border segmentation (80:20 split for training and validation).
use.other: Used for pre-segmentation of lesion borders to confine the analysis to BCC regions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the agreement between human annotators by calculating pairwise Jaccard indices on a subset of images annotated by 5 observers, establishing a baseline for human annotation consistency.
To summarize the distribution of agreement among the 5 human observers for vessel segmentation masks.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of model performance against human observer mean
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate whether the deep learning model's performance (Jaccard score) falls within the range of human observer variability.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It compared the deep learning model's performance (Jaccard score) against human observer variability using descriptive statistics (median pairwise Jaccard index) and direct metric comparison, concluding that the model's performance fell within the range of human variation.

Combined BCC Dataset (Test Set).notes: The U-Net model with Combo Loss achieved a Jaccard score of 37.8% (reported as 0.376 in Table 2) on the test set, which exceeded the mean Jaccard score among 5 human observers. The median pairwise Jaccard index among the 5 human observers was 0.271. The study notes that the model's performance falls within the variation of human observers.

Human Observer Variability (Inter-observ.notes: Analysis of 50 images annotated by 5 observers showed a median pairwise Jaccard index of 0.271. For specific example images, the mean Jaccard calculated for each pair of observers was 0.285. The study concludes that the deep learning model's results are more in agreement with each observer than the observers are with each other.

Comparison with Baseline (Kharazmi et al.notes: The paper compares its results to Kharazmi et al., noting that while Kharazmi's precision and recall were higher, the studies are not directly comparable because Kharazmi scored presence/absence of a vessel within a 32x32 pixel patch, whereas this study scored pixel-by-pixel presence/absence. Kharazmi's masks also included vascular structures other than vessels.

architecture_used.pretraining: The main vessel segmentation U-Net was not pre-trained on external weights; however, a separate U-Net was trained on the ISIC 2018 Task 1 Lesion Segmentation dataset (2594 images) to automatically determine lesion borders for confining the analysis to BCC regions.
architecture_used.input_size: 512 × 512 pixels (resized from original HAM10000 450×600 and NIH 768×1024 images). Varied input sizes for RGB channels (32, 64, 128, 256, 512) were used in the encoder/decoder paths.

architecture_details.structure: Standard U-Net with contractive (encoder) and expansive (decoder) paths. Encoder follows a convolutional neural network architecture downsampled 4 times. Decoder uses transposed 2D convolutional layers upsampled 4 times. Includes upsampling and concatenation with higher-resolution feature maps from the encoder. Final layer uses 1x1 convolution to map feature maps to classes.
architecture_details.activation_function: Exponential Linear Units (ELU) instead of traditional ReLU.
architecture_details.initialization: Truncated normal distribution centered on zero with standard deviation = sqrt(2/ fan_in).
architecture_details.regularization: Dropout layers with probability 0.1 in both encoder and decoder.
architecture_details.loss_function: Combination loss: Weighted sum of Dice loss and weighted binary cross-entropy (Combo Loss). Alpha for combo loss = 0.7. One weight = 0.89, Zero weight = 0.11.

training_details.early_stopping: Patience value of 5, saving the best model.
training_details.data_augmentation: Geometric (random rotation, horizontal flip, vertical flip) and Color (histogram stretching, CLAHE, normalization, brightness enhancement).

classification_or_output_head.output_layer: 1x1 convolution mapping to respective classes (vessel vs. skin)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Cheng et al. (Local pixel color drop technique)
Cheng et al. (Adaptive critic design approach)
Kharazmi et al. (Independent component analysis, k-means clustering, and shape filters)
preprocessing_techniques: Cropping nonsquare images to square dimensions
Geometric augmentations: random rotation, horizontal flip, and vertical flip
Color augmentations: histogram stretching for each color channel
Color augmentations: contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE)
Automatic lesion border segmentation using a separate U-Net trained on ISIC 2018 Task 1 dataset to confine analysis to BCC regions
Resizing ISIC 2018 images to 320 × 320 pixels using bilinear interpolation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random rotation, horizontal flip, and vertical flip.
Histogram stretching applied to each color channel, followed by contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE), normalization, and brightness enhancement.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning U-Net approach with a combination loss function (weighted binary cross-entropy and Dice loss) successfully automates the segmentation of telangiectasia (blood vessels) in dermoscopic images of basal cell carcinoma.

The model achieved a Jaccard score of 0.376 on the test set, which exceeds the mean Jaccard score among five human observers and falls within the range of human observer variability (median pairwise Jaccard index of 0.271).

The deep learning model's performance was more in agreement with each individual human observer than the observers were with each other, addressing the issue of inter-observer variability in annotation.

The study establishes a baseline method for pixel-based telangiectasia detection, demonstrating that automatic identification of cancer-specific structures is feasible despite severe class imbalance and annotation subjectivity.
The authors conclude that further application of deep learning techniques to detect dermoscopic structures and handle noisy labels is warranted for future research.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a small dataset of 690 basal cell carcinoma images (217 from HAM10000, 413 from NIH), which may restrict generalizability to broader skin cancer populations or other lesion types.

The model's performance (Jaccard index 0.376) is evaluated against human inter-observer variability (median pairwise Jaccard 0.271) rather than a gold standard, establishing a baseline relative to human annotation subjectivity rather than absolute ground truth accuracy.

There is significant inter-observer variability in the ground truth annotations, with a median pairwise Jaccard index of 0.271 among 5 observers, indicating that the 'ground truth' itself is noisy and subjective.

The paper acknowledges that previous comparisons with Kharazmi et al. are not directly comparable due to differences in scoring methodology (pixel-by-pixel vs. patch-based) and inclusion of non-vessel vascular structures in prior masks.

The study addresses severe class imbalance (vessels constitute only 2-10% of pixels) using a combination loss function (weighted binary cross-entropy and Dice loss), but high accuracy (0.987) may mask poor sensitivity to the minority class (Recall 0.521).

No external validation was performed; the model was trained and tested on a combined dataset from HAM10000 and NIH studies, limiting evidence of robustness across different clinical settings or image acquisition devices.

The study highlights the impact of software (Paint.net vs. Photoshop) and tool (stylus vs. mouse) variations on annotation consistency, suggesting that annotation protocols significantly influence model training data quality.
